note_3_3A_0.js.title: Gas Escape Trap — CP1 (Molar Volume)
note_3_3A_0.js.text: If the flask is not sealed immediately after adding Mg, hydrogen gas escapes before the bung seals. This causes the recorded volume to be too low, resulting in a calculated molar volume that is systematically lower than the expected ~24 dm³ mol⁻¹. Always pre-weigh the Mg and seal immediately.

note_3_3A_0.js.title: HCl Must Be in Excess — Examiner Requirement
note_3_3A_0.js.text: In CP1, examiners expect you to state explicitly that HCl must be in excess. Without this, not all Mg reacts, causing systematic underestimation of the molar volume. Check that the Mg dissolves completely — this confirms excess HCl.

note_3_3A_1.js.title: Missing Control Variables — Common Exam Mistake
note_3_3A_1.js.text: Many students describe apparatus and method steps but forget to identify control variables (what must be kept constant for a fair test). Examiners specifically look for these. Always explicitly state the independent, dependent, and control variables in your plan.

note_3_3A_1.js.title: Risk Assessment Must Link Hazard to Control Measure
note_3_3A_1.js.text: Saying 'wear goggles' or 'be careful' scores zero. Instead, write: 'Corrosive HCl (hazard) → wear nitrile gloves and safety goggles' (specific control). Link each hazard to a specific, actionable control measure.

note_3_3A_2.js.title: Burette Reading Precision — Common Exam Trap
note_3_3A_2.js.text: Recording burette readings to 1 decimal place (e.g. 24.1 cm³) loses marks. Burettes must be read to 2 decimal places (e.g. 24.10 cm³) because they are precise to ±0.05 cm³. Always record as 24.10, 0.00, 25.50 — never 24.1 or 0.0.

note_3_3A_2.js.title: Describe Initial → Final State in Observations
note_3_3A_2.js.text: Saying 'the solution turned yellow' is vague. Instead write: 'colourless solution turned yellow-orange' (initial → final). This shows you understand that chemical changes are measurable by comparing states, not just observing a single endpoint.

note_3_3A_3.js.title: Gradient Calculation — Use Best-Fit Line, Not Data Points
note_3_3A_3.js.text: A common mistake is calculating gradient using coordinates from actual data points: (x₁,y₁) to (x₂,y₂). You must instead use two points READ FROM YOUR SMOOTH BEST-FIT LINE. This gives a more representative gradient that avoids individual measurement errors.

note_3_3A_3.js.title: cm³ to dm³ Conversion — Major Calculation Trap
note_3_3A_3.js.text: Before using c = n/V, always convert volume to dm³. Forgetting to divide by 1000 (e.g. using 25 cm³ instead of 0.025 dm³) causes answers to be 1000× wrong. This is one of the most common calculation errors in mole questions.

note_3_3A_4.js.title: Burette Uncertainty × 2 — Critical Student Trap
note_3_3A_4.js.text: When calculating % uncertainty for a burette titre, you must multiply the instruments's uncertainty by 2 (not 1). This is because a titre requires TWO readings (initial and final). If the burette has ±0.05 cm³ precision, the total absolute uncertainty is ±0.10 cm³. Forgetting this doubles your % uncertainty calculation.

note_3_3A_4.js.title: Larger Measurements Give Lower % Uncertainty
note_3_3A_4.js.text: Percentage uncertainty = (absolute uncertainty / measurement) × 100. A 25 cm³ titre has lower % uncertainty than a 10 cm³ titre (both using ±0.10 cm³ absolute). This is why exam procedures specify using larger volumes and masses when possible — it improves experimental reliability.

note_3_3A_5.js.title: Over-Generalizing Conclusions — Validity Issue
note_3_3A_5.js.text: Conclusions must be supported by the data collected. Do not extend conclusions beyond the evidence. For example, if you test only one concentration, you cannot conclude that 'higher concentration always increases rate' — you can only state what happened in your experiment. State limitations explicitly.

note_3_3A_5.js.title: Explain WHY Further Investigations Are Needed
note_3_3A_5.js.text: When suggesting further work, examiners want to see that you understand what question it answers. Instead of 'repeat the experiment at higher temperature,' write: 'Investigate the effect of temperature on reaction rate (keeping concentration and surface area constant) to determine the activation energy of the reaction.' Always state the purpose.
